coredata.dc:title: Defect engineering-induced porosity in graphene quantum dots embedded metal-organic frameworks for enhanced benzene and toluene adsorption
coredata.dc:creator: Alivand, Masood S.
Tehrani, Neda Haj Mohammad Hossein
Askarieh, Mojtaba
Ghasemy, Ebrahim
Esrafili, Mehdi D.
Ahmadi, Raziyeh
Anisi, Hossein
Tavakoli, Omid
Rashidi, Alimorad
coredata.dc:description: The emerging environmental issues necessitate the engineering of novel and well-designed nanoadsorbents for advanced separation and purification applications. Despite recent advances, the facile synthesis of hierarchical micro-mesoporous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with tuned structures has remained a challenge. Herein, we report a simple defect engineering approach to manipulate the framework, induce mesoporosity, and crease large pore volumes in MIL-101(Cr) by embedding graphene quantum dots (GQDs) during its self-assembly process. For instance, MIL-101@GQD-3 (Vmeso: 0.68 and Vtot: 1.87 cm3/g) exhibited 300.0% and 53.3% more meso and total pore volume compared to those of the conventional MIL-101 (Vmeso: 0.17 and Vtot: 1.22 cm3/g), respectively, resulting in 1.7 and 2.8 times greater benzene and toluene loading at 1 bar and 25 °C. In addition, we found that MIL-101@GQD-3 retained its superiority over a wide range of VOC concentrations and operating temperature (25–55 °C) with great cyclic capacity and energy-efficient regeneration. Considering the simplicity of the adopted technique to induce mesoporosity and tune the nanoporous structure of MOFs, the presented GQD incorporation technique is expected to provide a new pathway for the facile synthesis of advanced materials for environmental applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of the MIL-101@GQD nanocomposites. (a) XRD patterns of typical MIL-101(Cr) and the prepared MIL-101@GQD nanoadsorbents. (b) FTIR spectra and (c) nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K, for GQD, MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD-3 samples. (d) Micropore and mesopore volume of the synthesized MIL-101(C) and MIL-101@GQD-3 nanoadsorbents. (e) TGA curves of GQD, MIL-101(Cr), and MIL-101@GQD-3. (f) The amount of weight reduction of MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD composites in the 300–600 °C range.

Effect of GQD incorporation on the MOF crystal formation. (a) Representative schematic, (b) crystal size distribution in nm and (c) SEM images of the formed MIL-101(Cr) crystals in (i) the control system (typical MIL-101(Cr) without GQD addition), (ii) MIL-101@GQD-1, (iii) MIL-101@GQD-2, (iv) MIL-101@GQD-3, and (v) MIL-101@GQD-4 with different GQD contents (from 1% to 4%, respectively). (d) The influence of steric hinderance caused by GQD nanoparticles on the coordination of MIL-101(Cr). SEM scale bars correspond to 500 nm.

(a) Representation of MIL-101@GQD crystals and the assembly of chromium ions and terephthalic acid molecules on the surface of GQD nanoparticles. (b) TEM images of MIL-101@GQD material. (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s and (d) EDAX mapping images of the MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD-3 materials showing Cr, N, and O elements.

Application of MIL-101@GQD nanomaterials. (a) Benzene and (d) toluene adsorption capacity of MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD. The relationship between equilibrium (b) benzene and (e) toluene gas adsorption capacity and total pore volume of MIL-101 (purple circle) and MIL-101@GQD materials (yellow, red, green, and blue circles represent 1–4 wt% GQD, respectively). (f) Benzene and (i) toluene breakthrough curves for MIL-101(black lines) and MIL-101@GQD-3 (red lines). (g-i) The equilibrium adsorption capacity of benzene (yellow bars) and toluene (gray bars) on MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD-3 at different concentrations (10, 50, and 100 vol%). All data were obtained at 25 °C and the equilibrium values were recorded after 90 min. Relative concentrations were reported compared to the saturated concentration of each gas in a binary mixture. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The calculated adsorption energy of (a) benzene and (b) toluene adsorbed on different prepared structures. The optimized geometry of (c, d) MIL-101(Cr), (e, f) MIL-101@GQD, and (g, h) MIL-101@GQD (Def) along with the most stable configurations of benzene and toluene molecules adsorbed on these substrates.
Illustration of the synthesis procedure and suggested formation mechanism of MIL-101@GQD network.
The comparison of surface area, total and mesopore volume, benzene and toluene uptake for different adsorbents. All the values have been reported in the absence of moisture.

head.ce:title: Defect engineering-induced porosity in graphene quantum dots embedded metal-organic frameworks for enhanced benzene and toluene adsorption

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emerging environmental issues necessitate the engineering of novel and well-designed nanoadsorbents for advanced separation and purification applications. Despite recent advances, the facile synthesis of hierarchical micro-mesoporous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with tuned structures has remained a challenge. Herein, we report a simple defect engineering approach to manipulate the framework, induce mesoporosity, and crease large pore volumes in MIL-101(Cr) by embedding graphene quantum dots (GQDs) during its self-assembly process. For instance, MIL-101@GQD-3 (Vmeso: 0.68 and Vtot: 1.87 cm3/g) exhibited 300.0% and 53.3% more meso and total pore volume compared to those of the conventional MIL-101 (Vmeso: 0.17 and Vtot: 1.22 cm3/g), respectively, resulting in 1.7 and 2.8 times greater benzene and toluene loading at 1 bar and 25 °C. In addition, we found that MIL-101@GQD-3 retained its superiority over a wide range of VOC concentrations and operating temperature (25–55 °C) with great cyclic capacity and energy-efficient regeneration. Considering the simplicity of the adopted technique to induce mesoporosity and tune the nanoporous structure of MOFs, the presented GQD incorporation technique is expected to provide a new pathway for the facile synthesis of advanced materials for environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are categorized among the harmful air pollutants with severe impacts on the ecological environment, human and animal health (Liu et al., 2016, 2019; Zhu et al., 2020). Although VOCs are widely used for the synthesis of industrial and domestic products, some handful of industries (e.g., coating, printing and fermentation) have been identified as the main sources of VOC emission (Liu et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2020). Considering the growing trend of these industries in both developed and developing countries, VOC removal at ambient conditions has attracted worldwide attention as an urgent problem (Yang et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2016; Cheng et al., 2020). Therefore, there is currently a high demand to introduce facile and applicable strategies to develop advanced VOC removal technologies, especially for benzene and toluene as the most dangerous airborne pollutants.

Among many different technologies used for the VOC removal from air stream (e.g., liquid absorption (Hsu and Lin, 2012), membrane separation (Ozturk et al., 2015), and catalytic oxidation (Xue et al., 2020)), the adsorption process has been a preferable technique for large-scale implementation, owing to its high capacity, long-term durability, flexibility and ease of operation (Tehrani et al., 2020). However, finding a suitable material with a facile synthesis procedure, high porosity, tunable pore structure and more importantly, inexpensive cost has remained a challenge. Many research efforts have been devoted to develop novel nanoadsorbents for VOC capture applications focusing on carbon-based materials (Tehrani et al., 2020; Ouzzine et al., 2019; Kante et al., 2019), zeolites (Li et al., 2018, 2020; Wang et al., 2019), polymers (Tan et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018b) and metal oxides (Adebayo et al., 2020a, 2020b). Recently, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have displayed an eminent performance for various applications (e.g., adsorption (Alivand et al., 2019; Mesgarian et al., 2020), electrocatalysis (Lu et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2020) and photocatalysis (Wang and Astruc, 2019; Xiao and Jiang, 2018)), particularly the adsorption of VOCs owing to their unique textural properties, low density, controllable pore size and high pore volume which have solidified the role of MOFs as a promising material (Wu et al., 2015; Gonzalez et al., 2018). Nevertheless, most of the stable MOFs are microporous structures which makes them less favorable for the adsorption of large VOC molecules. For example, MIL-101(Cr) is a well-known MOF with high surface area, large pore volume and low regeneration temperature. However, its microporous structure has made it inefficient for the VOC adsorption. Thus, it is imperative to engineer the pore structure of microporous MOFs to yield the best performance. ( Scheme 1).

In the efforts of developing micro-mesoporous MOFs, template synthesis has been an attractive strategy (Zhang et al., 2019c, 2019d). However, most of the used templates are expensive copolymers and trade-offs between facile synthesis procedure, eco-friendly nature, and inexpensive preparation cost remain serious constraints for this method. In addition, a limited number of templates have been reported to date and the achieved mesopore volume is not high enough. Hence, introducing a new strategy for the preparation of hierarchical micro-mesoporous MOFs is of great interest. Recently, we developed a facile methodology to prepare micro-mesoporous MIL-101(Cr) using a higher ligand to cluster ratio and investigated the hindering effect of excess ligand molecules during the hydrothermal synthesis of MIL-101(Cr) (Shafiei et al., 2018; Alivand et al., 2019). The prepared materials displayed a defective structure with high surface area and mesoporosity allowing for the fast and deep VOC uptake. Despite the successful creation of mesopores through the MIL-101(Cr) network and its excellent VOC removal performance, imposing steric hindrance by an excess amount of linker might increase the production cost. Therefore, it is important to investigate the ability of new nanoparticles for applying steric hindrance and controlled defect engineering through the self-assembly of MIL-101(Cr). Herein, for the first time, we used highly stabilized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a potential nanomaterial for the facile synthesis of hierarchical micro-mesoporous MIL-101@GQD nanocomposite and succeeded to effectively tune its VOC adsorption capabilities. GQDs are green and environment-friendly nanoparticles that can be easily and inexpensively synthesized and used as carboxyl-rich nucleation sites for the defective growth of MIL-101(Cr). We demonstrate how a small amount of GQD (i.e., 1–4 wt%) can cause steric hindrance, create structural defects, induce mesoporosity in MIL-101@GQD network and improve its benzene and toluene adsorption performance. The present investigation provides a clear insight into the tuning effect of GQDs for tailoring the self-assembly of MOF-based materials.

All chemicals in this research study were reagent grade and used as received without further modification. Citric acid (>99.5%), urea (99%), chromium nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3.9H2O, 97%) and ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 99.99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 1, 4-benzene dicarboxylic (H2BDC, 98%), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%), methanol (MeOH, 99.99%), ethanol (EtOH, 99.9%), and nitric acid (HNO3, 65%) were acquired from Merck. Benzene (99.8%, Merck) and toluene (99.8%, Merck) were used as VOC samples without additional treatments. Moreover, deionized (DI) water was utilized as a solvent for synthesis and purification purposes.

GQD was synthesized through the method previously described by our research group (Pourhashem et al., 2018). Briefly, 2.1 g of citric acid and 1.8 g of urea were mixed with 500 mL DI water and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Afterward, a Teflon-lined autoclave was employed to heat the prepared solution at 160 °C for 4 h. Finally, the colloidal GQDs were purified using a dialysis bag for 3 days and dried at 80 °C under vacuum overnight.

Crystalline MIL-101(Cr) was prepared according to our previous literature (Alivand et al., 2019). In summary, 8 g Cr(NO3)3.9H2O and 3.32 g H2BDC were dissolved in 100 mL DI water and stirred for 30 min. Then, 0.83 mL HNO3 was added dropwise to the solution to inhibit the fast deprotonation of carboxylic acid ligands during the self-assembly formation of crystals. The mixture was transferred into a hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 8 h. The obtained green crystals were separated using centrifugation (9000 rpm and 15 min) and the precipitants were vacuum dried at 150 °C overnight.

To purify the MIL-101 (Cr) crystals, the obtained samples were dispersed in hot DI water (200 mL, 90 °C and 2 h) and DMF (100 mL, 80 °C and 8 h) to remove unreacted chromium salt and H2BDC from the porous structure, respectively. The sample was dispersed in aqueous NH4F solution (100 mL, 30 mM, 60 °C and 5 h) to deeply evacuate micropores from unreacted H2BDC and then washed with a copious amount of DI water. Eventually, to replace the used DMF and water solvent, the sample was soaked in MeOH (50 mL, room temperature and 3 days) while the solvent changed every day. The final crystalline materials were filtered, dried at 150 °C under severe vacuum overnight and labeled as MIL-101(Cr).

To synthesize the MIL-101@GQD composite, a certain amount of GQD was added into 100 mL DI water and ultrasonicated for 30 min to fully disperse GQDs into the solution. Then, 8 g Cr(NO3)3.9H2O was added to the mixture and ultrasonicated for a further 30 min to bond metal ions with carboxylic acid groups of GQD. Afterward, 3.32 g H2BDC and 0.83 mL HNO3 were dissolved in the mixture and vigorously stirred for 30 min. The homogeneous solution was then transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 8 h. The MIL-101@GQD crystals were separated using a centrifuge (9000 rpm and 15 min) and vacuum dried at 150 °C overnight. To ensure the removal of unreacted H2BDC and other unwelcome components lying through the pores of MIL-101@GQD, the resulting sample was then post-purified using hot DMF, NH4F solution, hot DI water and methanol, respectively, according to the detailed procedure described for the synthesis of MIL-101(Cr). The amount of GQD added for the synthesis of MIL-101@GQD was 1, 2, 3 and 4 wt% of the initial reactants (i.e., Cr(NO3)3.9H2O and H2BDC). The resulted samples were labeled as MIL-101@GQD-X where X represents the amount of GQD in the composite (X = 1, 2, 3 and 4). For instance, MIL-101@GQD −3 refers to the hybrid MIL-101@GQD composite embedding 3 wt% GQD.

To get an insight into the chemical and morphological properties of synthesized nanoadsorbents thorough characterization techniques were employed. The crystallinity and the effect of GQD on the structure of MIL-101(Cr) were studied by performing the powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) using Philips PW-1730 instrument with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å and sweeping rate =1 deg/min). Chemical bonding of the synthesized samples was evaluated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) method via Perkin Elmer device with KBr pellets. The morphology of the nanoadsorbents was perused using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) by adopting Tescan, Mira II instrument. The EOLJEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) device was utilized to take the detailed micrographs of MIL-101@GQD samples. Also, the surface properties and porosity of all prepared samples were investigated by nitrogen adsorption-desorption technique at 77.3 K using Micrometrics apparatus (ASAP 2020). Before performing the nitrogen adsorption-desorption, the samples were degassed at 140 °C for 48 h. To achieve the specific surface area, the Brunauer, Emmett and Teller (BET) method was considered and the pore diameter was obtained by using the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method. The effect of GQD on the thermal stability of the MIL-101 (Cr) was studied by utilizing the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) under continuous nitrogen flow and 5 °C/min heating rate. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized using VG ESCALAB 220i-XL spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with a constant photon energy of 1486.6 eV to evaluate the surface properties of the prepared MIL-101@GQD materials. The carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen and sulfur elements were quantitatively measured using Eager 300 EA 1112 elemental analyzer.

Adsorption of benzene and toluene onto the prepared nanomaterials at 25 °C and 1 bar was carried out according to our previous works (Tehrani et al., 2020; Shafiei et al., 2018) using an in-house built VOC adsorption rig (Fig. S1). Briefly, the designed rig was composed of three main parts; i) gas saturation tube, ii) U-shape adsorption column, and iii) temperature controller system. Before performing the adsorption experiments, the desired adsorbent was degassed at 150 °C under severe vacuum for at least 12 h, weighed, loaded into the U-shape column and connected to the rig. Then, the gas saturation tube was filled with liquid benzene/toluene, connected to the system and completely sealed. To begin the adsorption experiment, standard dry air was continuously supplied as a carrier gas (MFC-1, 120 mL/min). Then, the air stream was passed toward the gas saturation tube, mixed with benzene/toluene and left the glassy saturation tube. The VOC-saturated stream was passed through the U-shape adsorption column and captured by the loaded sample. The flow direction in the U-shape adsorption column was considered downward to inhibit the fluidization of the loaded sample and provide enough residence time. Considering the significant difference in the molecular weight of benzene/toluene and air, it was assumed that only VOC molecules can be adsorbed on the sample and the effect of air adsorption was neglected. To control the temperature of the gas saturation tube and U-shape adsorption column, an external circulator containing water-ethylene glycol mixture (40:60) was utilized during VOC adsorption experiments with ±0.1 °C accuracy.

The breakthrough adsorption curves were determined by recording the samples’ weight at different time intervals. When the sample weight reached almost a constant value (±0.5%) at three successive steps, the process was finished and the final value was reported as the equilibrium adsorption capacity. To calculate the amount of adsorbed VOC, the following equation was utilized:

To test the dynamic adsorption capacity of prepared materials at different concentrations of benzene/toluene (i.e., 10, 50 and 100 vol%), a proper amount of dry air stream (supplied by MFC-2) was mixed with VOC-saturated stream. It is worth mentioning that 100 vol% concentration represents the saturation concentration of the benzene/toluene at air stream and other concentrations have been defined based on this value as a baseline. For cyclic adsorption-desorption experiments, the air flow rate stopped at the end of the adsorption experiment and then it was replaced with pure nitrogen (MFC-3, 100 mL/min). To reverse the equilibrium conditions and provide good desorption kinetics, the temperature was set to 120 °C for 4 h. After a complete regeneration and before starting the next cycle, the temperature decreased again to 25 °C and the system was purged with dry air to completely remove the remained nitrogen and other unwelcome gas molecules.

The DFT calculations were performed by the Dmol3 program (Delley, 1990, 2000) using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzernhof (PBE) density functional (Perdew et al., 1997). A double numerical polarization (DNP) basis set was used to expand the atomic wave functions. The Grimme's DFT-D method (Grimme, 2004, 2006) was adopted to treat the so-called "dispersion effects" in the benzene or toluene adsorption complexes. Previous studies (Mudchimo et al., 2018; Esrafili, 2019) have demonstrated that the combination of this method with a DNP basis set is sufficient to properly describe the geometric and electronic structure properties of weakly bonded complexes. During the geometry relaxation, all atoms were allowed to relax without any symmetry constraints. A convergence tolerance of 0.001 Ha/Å, 0.005 Å, and 10−5 Ha was set to max force, max displacement and change energy, respectively.

The GQD was stimulated by a supercell containing 110 carbon atoms. Dangling bonds of edge carbon atoms were avoided by adding hydrogen atoms. To evaluate the adsorption of benzene and toluene on the GQD, adsorption energy (Eads) was obtained by the following equation:

are the total energies of benzene/toluene adsorbed on GQD, isolated benzene/toluene molecule and GQD, respectively. According to this definition, a negative Eads value corresponds to an exothermic adsorption.

The XRD pattern of the prepared MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD materials are illustrated in Fig. 1. The XRD pattern of the parent MIL-101(Cr) structure complies well with its simulated XRD pattern and those reported in the literature, suggesting the successful formation of MIL-101(Cr) structure(Zhou et al., 2014; Yan et al., 2014; Sarker et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2018a). As can be noticed, all MIL-101@GQD nanocomposites exhibited nearly the same XRD pattern similar to that of the parent MIL-101(Cr) which confirms that the crystalline structure of parent MIL-101(Cr) was preserved in hybrid MIL-101@GQD materials. It should be also noted that MIL-101@GQD composites did not show any characteristic peaks related to the GQD which is attributed to the i) extremely small size of GQDs (around 0.2–2.0 nm) compared to the large crystals of MIL-101(Cr), ii) the amorphous nature of GQDs, and iii) their excellent dispersion in MIL-101(Cr) structure. The introduction of GQD into the MIL-101(Cr) structure gradually increased the intensity of peaks and reached its maximum value in the MIL-101@GQD-3 sample. According to the findings of Zhou et al. (2014), the preparation of MIL-101@Graphene Oxide (GO) nanocomposite can significantly increase the intensity of MIL-101(Cr) peaks in XRD characterization. However, the physical mixing of MIL-101(Cr)/GO resulted in a decrease in the intensity of peaks. Therefore, it can be inferred that GQDs were successfully embedded in the MIL-101(Cr) structure and formed a homogeneous nanocomposite. Contrarily, by further raising the GQD content in MIL-101@GQD-4, the peak intensity was reduced implying that some GQDs were physically mixed into the MIL-101(Cr) structure.

To identify the bonds and functional groups of GQD, the parent MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101 @GQD composites, FTIR spectroscopy was utilized (Figs. 1b and S2). For GQD, C-H peaks were detected at 764, 1263 and 1457 cm−1, while 1165 and 1457 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching of C-O and symmetric N-O. For MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101 @GQD, the stretching vibration of Cr-O was emerged at around 570 cm−1. All the bands in the 750–1160 cm−1 range and 1508 cm−1 were attributed to the C-H bonds of H2BDC and C˭C vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, the appeared peaks at 1404 and 1625 cm−1 were related to the symmetric stretching of the carboxyl bond (-COO-) (Sarker et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018a; Chen et al., 2019; Ahmed and Jhung, 2016; Prabhakaran and Deschamps, 2015). As it is evident, all MIL-101@GQD samples had basically the same FTIR spectra and the addition of GQD did not have a visible influence on the general composition and functional groups of the MIL-101(Cr) crystals. The only difference can be observed at 1710 cm−1 peak originating from C˭O bond of the carboxyl group which was disappeared in MIL-101@GQD. Since the edges of GQD are full of oxygenated functional groups, it can easily react with carboxyl groups of MIL-101(Cr) and form irregular chains with dissociated C˭O/C-O. Based on this result, we can deduce that GQDs can be actively incorporated in the self-assembly and crystal formation mechanism of MIL-101(Cr). This is in line with the XRD patterns (Fig. 1a) which reconfirms that hybrid MIL-101@GQD materials thoroughly retained MIL-101(Cr) structure and GQDs just embedded into the MIL-101(Cr) cages and pores.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the parent MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD composites are illustrated in Figs. 1c and S3a. For MIL-101(Cr), type-I isotherm was observed, representing microporous structures, while type-IV isotherm appeared for MIL-101@GQD composites with a hysteresis loop at high relative pressures (P/P0 > 0.9), implying the formation of a hierarchical micro-mesoporous structure. Table S1 provides details of the textural properties of MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD materials. It is worth noting that a negligible porosity was recorded for GQD. The pristine MIL-101(Cr) sample possessed a specific surface area and pore volume of 2646 m2/g and 1.22 cm3/g with a predominant micropore volume. The specific surface area and pore volume increased to 2977 m2/g and 1.69 cm3/g in MIL-101@GQD-1, respectively. As observed in Table S1, it is evident that this improvement is related to the accelerated growth of both micropores and mesopores in the MIL-101@GQD structure. The mesopore and total pore volume of MIL-101@GQD consistently improved with an increase in GQD content and reached 0.68 and 1.87 cm3/g in MIL-101@GQD-3, which are 300% and 53.3% greater than those of the parent MIL-101(Cr), respectively (Fig. 1d). The development of mesopores in the microporous structure of MIL-101(Cr) resulted in increasing the share of mesoporosity (i.e., Vmeso/Vtotal) from 14% in MIL-101(Cr) to 39% in MIL-101@GQD-3. It was also observed that MIL-101@GQD materials show a higher average pore diameter than MIL-101(Cr) which was reconfirmed by pore size distribution graphs (Fig. S3b). Particularly, the average pore diameters of MIL-101@GQD-3 was 2.69 nm, which is 45.4% more than that of MIL-101(Cr). These results confirm that the embedding of GQDs during the self-assembly of MIL-101@GQD can highly control the crystalline structure of MIL-101 and its pore development mechanism, from highly microporous to micro-mesoporous structure. However, introducing a higher quantity of GQD resulted in a significant reduction of the textural properties in MIL-101@GQD-4 which is mainly due to the partial distortion and blockage of porous medium and channels which cannot be compensated by the creation of new pores. Regarding the negligible porosity of GQD, it can be deduced that MIL-101@GQD materials benefit from a new mesoporosity on the interface between GQD and MOF blocks. Since GQD is fully rich in carboxylic acid groups, especially at the edges, it is highly prone to compete with H2BDC linkers, change the coordination between oxygen groups and Cr3+ metal centers, and create a hierarchical micro-mesoporous structure. Hence, it can be inferred that adding GQD into the MIL-101(Cr) structure combined a series of micropore channels and formed a new range of mesopores with higher pore diameter in MIL-101@GQD composites. This can be a determining step for advanced engineering of porosity in MIL-101(Cr) to improve gas adsorption capabilities.

The thermal stability of the prepared MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD materials was studied by TGA (Figs. 1e and S4). The weight loss of the parent MIL-101(Cr) can be categorized into three stages; 25–110 °C, 110–300 °C, and 300–600 °C. The first two stages are alluded to the desorption of physically adsorbed water/solvent molecules from the large and middle-size cages of the framework, respectively. The final stage was originated from the decomposition of the H2BDC linker, resulting in the severe disassembly of the MIL-101(Cr) structure (Férey et al., 2005; Hong et al., 2009; Jhung et al., 2007; Qasem et al., 2017). Interestingly, although MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD materials reflected the same weight reduction in the first two stages, we found that adding GQD led to a remarkable improvement in the thermal stability of MIL-101@GQD in 300–600 °C range (Fig. 1f). The lower weight reduction of MIL-101@GQD than that of parent MIL-101(Cr) in this temperature range can be attributed to the missing-linker defects and enhanced stability of H2BDC linker caused by the higher bonding strength of GQD. Furthermore, the GQD-incorporated MIL-101(Cr) crystals had a good thermal energy absorbance which could effectively suppress the H2BDC vibration, especially at high temperatures. Since there is a linear relationship between the weight loss of materials in 300–600 °C and the amount of linker in the framework, changing the weight reduction value from 60.13 in MIL-101–36.31–40.38 in MIL-101@GQD represents a significant reduction of Cr:H2BDC ratio in the MOF structure from 1:1 in MIL-101–1:0.6 in MIL-101@GQD-3.

The morphology of the parent MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD crystals was investigated by SEM analysis ( Figs. 2 and S5). Fig. 2 clearly shows an octahedral crystal shape for all MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD materials, where no sign of agglomerated GQD was found on the external surface of crystals. These results match well with those of the XRD and BET analysis, reconfirming the homogeneous dispersion of GQD in the MIL-101@GQD network. As it can be noticed from Fig. 2a–c, the average crystal size gradually decreased with the increase in the GQD content, from approximately 212 nm in pristine MIL-101(Cr) to 66 nm in MIL-101@GQD-4. Thus, we deduce that the observed crystal size reduction was caused by the inhibitory role of GQD during the self-assembly of MOF crystals, which is consistent with the findings of FTIR evaluation. Taking the results of both SEM and BET analysis into consideration, we found that the steric hindrance caused by GQDs imposed a constraint towards the degree of freedom for MIL-101(Cr) crystal growth (Fig. 2d). As a result, the appeared defects in the coordination of metal-ligand centers not only can prevent the large crystal formation but also can broaden the micropores and create a hierarchical network.

To further investigate the morphology of MIL-101@GQD-3, TEM analysis was performed ( Fig. 3). The low-magnification images (Fig. 3i and ii) clearly demonstrate that the polyhedral crystals of MIL-101(Cr) were preserved in the MIL-101@GQD-3. On the other hand, the embedded GQDs can be well observed in high-magnification TEM images with a spherical shape (0.2–3.0 nm) within the MIL-101(Cr) crystals. The d-spacing of the GQDs was determined by employing the ImageJ software as 0.246 nm, corresponding to the (1120) plane of the hexagonal close packed (HCP) crystals of GQD (Dey et al., 2018). The dark spots in Fig. 3iii–vi are attributed to the GQDs which formed due to the interactions between transmitting electrons and the layers of GQD structure. Considering the abundance of carboxyl groups on the external surface of GQDs, they can efficiently act as nucleation sites for the self-assembly of MIL-101(Cr) (Fig. 3a). Accordingly, MIL-101(Cr) crystals are grown over GQDs which can induce a substantial strain to cages and windows of the MIL-101(Cr), stress the structure and create small crystals with some defects in the assembled network. As reported by Qiu et al. (2015), two-dimensional GO sheets can promote the nucleation of MOFs and improve their morphological structure. In this regard, GQDs with a smaller size, greater surface area and higher surface energy can be more efficient in the nucleation of MIL-101(Cr). To investigate the coordination of GQD species on the external surface of MIL-101@GQD-3 crystals, XPS analysis was used. Although the high-resolution C1s and O1s XPS spectra of MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD-3 were the same, the trace of GQD appeared in the high-resolution N1s spectra of MIL-101@GQD-3 (Fig. 3c). The MIL-101(Cr) structure only exhibited a peak at 404.7 eV which corresponds to the nitro functional group. Contrarily, MIL-101@GQD-3 displayed an additional peak at the binding energy of 399.2 eV which is ascribed to the pyridinic N of the GQD structure. The presence of more N elements on the surface of MIL-101@GQD-3 crystals was also confirmed through EDAX mapping (Fig. 3d) and elemental analysis (Table S2).

Since changing the amount of GQD can leverage and engineer the porosity of MIL-101@GQD materials with different performances and capabilities, it is of great interest to determine the optimum surface properties for enhanced adsorption of specific molecules. Thus, the benzene and toluene adsorption performance of all synthesized MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD materials were tested at ambient conditions (25 °C and 1 bar; Figs. S6, S7). The results illustrated in Fig. 4a and d suggest that MIL-101@GQD materials exhibit superior benzene and toluene adsorption capacity. Interestingly, the results display that the benzene and toluene adsorption capacity of MIL-101@GQD-3 was 215.4 wt% and 268.1 wt%, nearly 1.72 and 2.75 times greater than those of the parent MIL-101(Cr). As can be noticed in Fig. 4b and d, there is a direct relationship between the gas uptake capacity and the pore volume of materials, revealing the determining influence of both microporosity and mesoporosity on benzene/toluene adsorption. This shows that the higher hierarchical micro-mesopore volume of MIL-101@GQD-3 material, as well as its uncoordinated metal sites resulted by the missing-linker deficiency, can be highlighted as the predominant factor for its extra-high benzene and toluene adsorption capacity. It was also reconfirmed by a simple comparison between the performance of MIL-101@GQD-3 and other novel nanomaterials reported in literature ( Table 1). As seen, MIL-101(Cr) can display a better performance than zirconium-based MOFs, like UiO-66 and UiO-67, and their modified structures, owing to the higher pore volume. On the other hand, the carbon-based materials with better textural properties than MIL-101@GQD-3 exhibited less values for gas adsorption capacity. This superiority originates from the interaction between the benzene/toluene molecules and active metal sites throughout the defected structure of MIL-101@GQD-3. Notably, the current modulated self-assembly of MIL-101(Cr) using the active incorporation of GQDs is a simple and environmentally friendly method compared with the typical defect-induced methods or post-functionalization strategies.

The breakthrough adsorption curves of benzene and toluene onto the MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD demonstrate that a majority of gas uptake takes place in the initial 10–20 min (Fig. 4c and f). While benzene/toluene molecules are adsorbed through the microporous structure of MIL-101(Cr), MIL-101@GQD provides a higher space through its micro-mesoporous structure, resulting in a steeper increase in the gas adsorption in the beginning of the gas adsorption process. In addition to the special physical characteristics and engineered pore structure, the embedded GQDs can improve the electron distribution and polarity on the internal surface of the porous structure which creates p- π bonds, increasing the surface energy and improving adsorption affinity of benzene/toluene on MIL-101@GQD composite (Zhang and Song, 2017). Hence, we believe that the enhanced kinetics of gas uptake in MIL-101@GQD compared to those of the parent MIL-101(Cr) is related to its engineered pore structure and the quantum confinement effect of GQD leading to a more favorable benzene/toluene adsorption.

To simulate the performance of the synthesized materials in actual conditions, benzene and toluene adsorption experiments were carried out at different gas concentrations (Figs. 4g–i and S8). As displayed in Fig. 4g–i, the engineered MIL-101@GQD-3 sample retained its superiority over the parent MIL-101(Cr) even at very low concentrations. Interestingly, MIL-101@GQD-3 displayed a high benzene and toluene adsorption capacity of 118.9 and 153.1 wt% at only 10 vol% concentration, respectively, while the parent MIL-101(Cr) was nearly ineffective at the same diluted condition. Since the single-layer gas adsorption is the main mechanism at low benzene/toluene concentrations, micropores determine the overall gas uptake performance of nanomaterials. However, in this case, MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD nanomaterials with almost similar micropore volumes exhibited remarkably different gas uptakes. It indicates that the inner mesopores of MIL-101@GQD-3 had a good affinity toward benzene/toluene molecules even at low concentrations which might be attributed to the quantum confinement effect of GQDs decorated in the quantum box of MIL-101(Cr). Accordingly, the total pore volume of MIL-101@GQD-3 could be accessible for benzene/toluene adsorption at a wide range of concentrations from 10 to 100 vol%.

To better understand the adsorption mechanism of benzene and toluene over the synthesized MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD materials, their gas uptake capacity was measured at different operating temperatures (Fig. S9). As expected, it was observed that the increase in the adsorption temperature from 25 to 55 °C reduced the gas uptake capacity of materials, suggesting that the benzene/toluene adsorption is mainly governed by physisorption. The physical nature of benzene and toluene adsorption on MIL-101 @GQD materials was also confirmed by DFT simulation presented later. It was revealed that the operating temperature has a significant effect on the benzene and toluene adsorption of MIL-101@GQD-3 nanoadsorbent, showing around 55.6% and 60.3% reduction for benzene (215.4–95.7 wt%) and toluene (268.1–106.5 wt%) adsorption capacity, respectively, when adsorption temperature increased from 25 to 55 °C. On the contrary, it was noticed that the parent MIL-101(Cr) exhibits a better gas uptake stability during a similar temperature increase. This can be directly ascribed to the nanoporous structure and pore size distribution of materials. In MIL-101(Cr), benzene/toluene molecules are dominantly adsorbed in single layers through the microporous structure with a strong affinity to the walls. In contrast, a large portion of gas molecules is adsorbed in mesopores of MIL-101@GQD with multi-layer adsorption and pore-filling mechanism, resulting in a weaker affinity of molecules to the surface. Taking these results into consideration, it can be stated that this unique property of MIL-101@GQD materials can be a privilege in the synthesis of energy-efficient nanoadsorbents. Hence, we investigated the benzene/toluene adsorption-desorption of both MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101 @GQD-3 materials over three consecutive cycles. As demonstrated in Fig. S10, MIL-101@GQD-3 exhibited 93.88% and 94.22% release of benzene and toluene, respectively, as the operating temperature increased from 25 to 120 °C, which are much greater than those of the parent MIL-101(Cr). The cyclic capacity of MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD-3 was also examined, where the incorporation of GQDs into MIL-101@GQD-3 sample led to 4.23% and 4.12% reduction in the adsorption capacity of benzene and toluene over three successive cycles, while 20.04% and 10.13% were recorded for the parent MIL-101(Cr). Based on the above observations, it can be concluded that the newly synthesized MIL-101@GQD-3 with engineered pore structure can be used as a favorable nanoadsorbent with higher cyclic capacity, longer lifetime and lower energy consumption.

We also performed DFT calculations to gain a better insight into the benzene/toluene adsorption mechanism on the synthesized MIL-101(Cr) and MIL-101@GQD materials. To simulate the MIL-101@GQD substrate, several configurations were considered. According to our DFT calculations, the most stable configuration of MIL-101@GQD is characterized by a minimum binding distance of 3.48 Å between a Cr atom and GQD. The adsorption energy of MIL-101(Cr) on GQD is −7.40 kcal/mol indicating the weak interaction between these species. As a result, the positive atomic charge on the Cr atom remains almost unchanged due to the adsorption over GQD. To study the surface reactivity of MIL-101(Cr) or MIL-101@GQD substrates, a single benzene/toluene molecule was located on the active sites of these materials ( Fig. 5c–h). The obtained adsorption energies (Eads) and charge-transfer (qCT) values are also illustrated in Fig. 5a, b and Table S3.

Since MIL-101(Cr) interacts with benzene/toluene through its Cr atom, the adsorption of these molecules is mainly governed by metal-π type interaction, in which benzene/toluene molecule acts as a π-donor. This finding is also verified by the Hirshfeld atomic charge analysis, in which a negligible charge shifts from benzene/toluene moiety into the substrate. The Eads of benzene is calculated to be −7.48 kcal/mol, which is slightly smaller than that of toluene (−7.92 kcal/mol). This is consistent with the results of other DFT studies (Akay et al., 2019; Björk et al., 2010) and can be mainly related to the more favorable π-electron donor characteristic of toluene compared to benzene. In fact, the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis of these species indicated a larger negative electrostatic potential associated with the ring carbon atoms of toluene (Fig. S11). As a result, toluene tends to establish stronger metal-π interaction with the MIL-101(Cr). This is consistent with the results of other DFT studies (Akay et al., 2019; Björk et al., 2010) and can be mainly related to the more favorable π-electron donor characteristic of toluene compared to benzene. According to the molecular orbital analysis in Figs. S12 and 13, it can be realized that the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of both benzene/MIL-101(Cr) and toluene/MIL-101(Cr) complexes is largely localized on Cr metal centers which is mostly attributed to the 3dz2 orbital of these atoms. On the other hand, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is dominantly non-bonding orbitals of the central O atom with some contributions from Cr atoms. These results, as well as the small adsorption energy (Eads) and charge-transfer (qCT) values suggest that physisorption is the predominant adsorption mechanism of benzene and toluene molecules on the parent MIL-101(Cr) substrate.

To probe how the presence of GQD improves the adsorption properties of benzene and toluene, the Eads and qCT values of the corresponding optimized MIL-101@GQD structure were calculated (Fig. 5a, b and e–h). Although the adsorption configuration of benzene and toluene remains almost unchanged, however, it is evident that the incorporation of GQD tends to improve the Eads values compared to those of parent MIL-101(Cr). The result of charge density analysis also verifies this finding, where the modification of MIL-101(Cr) leads to a negligible increase in the amount of qCT value. Given the carbon monovacancy as a common defect in graphene or GQD (Kakaei et al., 2019), we also considered the adsorption behavior of benzene/toluene on MIL-101@GQD (Def). For this aim, a carbon monovacancy defect is first created on GQD and then MIL-101(Cr) was allowed to relax on the defective GQD (Fig. 5g, h). The introduction of a carbon monovacancy in GQD tends to localize a large electron density around the defective area and strengthen the charge-transfer effect between MIL-101(Cr) and GQD (Def) species. Consequently, the adsorption energy and surface charge are improved in MIL-101@GQD (Def), resulting in a higher affinity and superior benzene/toluene adsorption. It should be also noted that both Eads and qCT values associated with toluene adsorption are slightly greater than those of benzene which is consistent with the experimental observations discussed above.

In summary, a general strategy was developed for defect engineering and tuning the pore structure of MOFs using ultra-small GQDs. The effects of GQD incorporation during the self-assembly of MIL-101(Cr) on the physicochemical properties of the prepared MIL-101@GQD materials were investigated. The formation of smaller crystal size and hierarchical micro-mesoporous structure appeared to be the main influence of GQD on the synthesized MIL-101@GQD structure. To demonstrate the potential application of MIL-101@GQD materials, their benzene and toluene adsorption behavior were examined at different gas concentrations and operating temperatures. The MIL-101@GQD-3 nanoadsorbent exhibited excellent adsorption capacity, recyclability, and ease of regeneration compared to those of the parent MIL-101(Cr), which are attributed to the large pore volume with tuned intra-connected hierarchical micro-mesoporosity and the high affinity of GQDs decorated through the porous structure. Overall, the present work not only attests to the success of GQD incorporated defect engineering as a facile approach in tuning the structure of MOFs, but also provides a valuable insight into the formation of MOF@GQD materials and their potential gas uptake mechanisms.
